paragraphs.text: Here’s a neat rare-earth story from **NIST SRD-46**: **the lanthanide contraction shows up very cleanly in EDTA binding**.

claims.text: **the lanthanide contraction shows up very cleanly in EDTA binding**

La^[3+] | Ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid (EDTA) | [ML]/[M][L] | K | 15.36 | 25.0 | 0.1 | H_[2]O … Lu^[3+] | … | K | 19.74 | 25.0 | 0.1 | H_[2]O

paragraphs.text: At **25.0 °C**, **ionic strength = 0.1 mol/L**, in **water (H₂O)**, the 1:1 EDTA complex formation constant **K = [ML]/[M][L]** rises steadily across the trivalent rare earths:

claims.text: rises steadily across the trivalent rare earths

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: ionic_strength_mol_l = 0.1 for EDTA complexes at 25.0 °C in water (e.g., La^[3+])
solvent_name = H_[2]O for the EDTA (1:1) constants at 25.0 °C, I=0.1 (e.g., La^[3+])
Ligand EDTA with beta_definition_name [ML]/[M][L] indicates the 1:1 formation constant
beta_definition_name = [ML]/[M][L] with constant_type = K for EDTA complexes (e.g., La^[3+])
La 15.36 < Nd 16.51 < Gd 17.35 < Er 18.89 < Lu 19.74 (25.0 °C, I=0.1, H2O) shows increasing K across trivalent lanthanides

paragraphs.text: **Why this is interesting:** as you go from **La³⁺ to Lu³⁺**, the ions get smaller while staying 3+, so their charge density increases. EDTA binds the later lanthanides much more strongly: **Lu³⁺ (19.74)** is about **4.38 log units** stronger than **La³⁺ (15.36)** under the same conditions.

claims.text: as you go from **La³⁺ to Lu³⁺**,
EDTA binds the later lanthanides much more strongly:

paragraphs.text: A couple of other fun SRD-46 observations:

claims.text: A couple of other fun SRD-46 observations:

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.

paragraphs.text:
| Observation | What SRD-46 shows |
|---|---|
| Rare-earth chemistry is mostly trivalent | The database coverage is overwhelmingly for **REE³⁺** species; non-3+ entries are scarce. |
| Oxidation-state exceptions stand out | **Ce** appears as **Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺**, and **Eu** shows **Eu²⁺/Eu³⁺/Eu⁴⁺** entries, making them notable outliers. |
| Ligand usage is repetitive across the series | Many rare earths share the same recurring ligands, including **EDTA**, **NTA**, **glycolic acid**, **malonic acid**, and **hydrogen sulfate**. |

claims.text: | Rare-earth chemistry is mostly trivalent | The database coverage is overwhelmingly for **REE³⁺** species; non-3+ entries are scarce. |
| Oxidation-state exceptions stand out | **Ce** appears as **Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺**, and **Eu** shows **Eu²⁺/Eu³⁺/Eu⁴⁺** entries, making them notable outliers. |
| Ligand usage is repetitive across the series | Many rare earths share the same recurring ligands, including **EDTA**, **NTA**, **glycolic acid**, **malonic acid**, and **hydrogen sulfate**. |

paragraphs.text: So the “interesting thing” is that rare earths may look chemically similar, but the database shows a **strong, systematic increase in chelate stability across the series**—a textbook periodic trend captured numerically.

claims.text: rare earths may look chemically similar
the database shows a **strong, systematic increase in chelate stability across the series**
